coredata.dc:title: Excitation-independent carbon dot probes for exogenous and endogenous Fe3+ sensing in living cells: Fluorescence lifetime and sensing mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Lesani, Pooria
Ardekani, Sara Madadi
Dehghani, Alireza
Hassan, Mahbub
Gomes, Vincent G.
coredata.dc:description:
Ferric ions play significant roles in human bodily functions, since both overload and deficiency can cause major physiological dysfunctions. Hence, engineering of sensitive, selective sensors to detect such ions merits attention. We found that certain CDs derived from a nitrogen-containing precursor (phthalocyanine) in suitable media could provide significant advantages. Excitation-independent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared through facile one-pot hydrothermal (W-CDs) and solvothermal (E-CDs) methods. Compared to W-CDs, ethanol-mediated E-CDs provide narrower particle size distribution, higher pyrrolic and pyridinic N-content, higher quantum yield (
Q Y
E - C D s
/
Q Y
W - C D s
= 2.58), strong green luminescence, excellent biocompatibility and efficient two-photon absorption. The E-CDs enabled superior NIR excitable two-photon bioimaging of breast cancer cells at variable depths and fluorescence quenching (81% extinction) for 10–600 μM Fe3+ ions. The mechanism of sensing was elucidated based on the fluorescence lifetime and the Stern-Volmer formalism. Intracellular Fe3+ ion sensing was first confirmed with E-CDs for lysed MCF-7 cells and quenching was detected in direct proportion to Fe3+ concentration (0–80 μM test range) with an LOD of 10.8 nM. Next, MCF-7 cells supplemented with variable concentrations of Fe3+ were incubated with E-CDs for sensing intracellular Fe3+ in live cells. The E-CDs showed high sensitivity for sensing endogenous Fe3+ ions in a stress-induced cell-based model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cell bioimaging
Intracellular sensing
Ferric ion
Fluorescence lifetime

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of endogenous Fe3+ ions in living cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Sensing principle of nanoprobes for Fe3+.
TEM images (a, c) and particle size distributions (b, d) of W-CDs and E-CDs, respectively.
(a) FT-IR spectra of E-CDs, W-CDs, and PHTH, (b) Raman spectra of E-CDs and W-CDs.
XPS full scan spectrum (a, b), C1s spectra (c, d), N1s spectra (e, f), and O1s spectrum (g, h) of E-CDs and W-CDs, respectively.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of W-CDs (dotted line) and E-CDs (solid line); (b) and (c) FL spectra of W-CDs and E-CDs, respectively; (d) The photoluminescence excitation spectra of E-CDs; (e) Two-photon emission spectra of E-CDs in the excitation range of 720–840 nm; (f) Quadratic relationship between measured fluorescence intensity of E-CDs in aqueous solution with respect to excitation laser power at 780 nm (PEX , as measured at the focal plane).
(a) Two-photon fluorescence and (b) bright-field images of MCF-7 stained with 200 μg/mL E-CDs for 24 h, (c) the merged image of (a) and (b). Scale: 20 μm.

(a) The selectivity of E-CDs to various metal ions (500 μM), (b) fluorescence emission spectra of the E-CDs in the presence of variable Fe3+ concentration, (c) fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 as a function of Fe3+ concentration, and (d) Stern-Volmer plot describes the fluorescence quenching intensity vs. the Fe3+ concentration over the range.0–600 μM.
Intracellular fluorescence emission spectra of lysed MCF-7 cells cultured with E-CDs in the presence of different concentration of Fe3+.

Two-photon confocal microscopy images of intracellular exogenous Fe3+ in MCF-7 cells after 24 h incubation with 200 μg/mL E-CDs and then incubated with 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μM Fe3+ for 1 h, respectively. MCF-7 cells incubated with 200 μg/mL E-CDs and then incubated with equal volume cell culture medium instead of metal ions as the control group. Images of brightfield microscopy (A–F), fluorescence microscopy (G–L) and overlay (M–R). Scale: 10 μm.

Real time two-photon confocal microscopy of intracellular endogenous Fe3+ in MCF-7 cells treated with 200 μg/mL E-CDs for 24 h and AA (1 mM) for 1 h followed by LPS treatment and imaged after: 0 min (A, F, K); 10 min (B, G, L); 20 min (C, H, M); 30 min (D, I, N); 40 min (E, J, O). Scale: 10 μm.
Concentrations of C, O, and N in E-CDs and W-CDs from XPS.
Fluorescence lifetimes of E-CDs with variable Fe3+ concentration.

head.ce:title: Excitation-independent carbon dot probes for exogenous and endogenous Fe3+ sensing in living cells: Fluorescence lifetime and sensing mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The University of Sydney, School of Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering, NSW 2006, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Ferric ions play significant roles in human bodily functions, since both overload and deficiency can cause major physiological dysfunctions. Hence, engineering of sensitive, selective sensors to detect such ions merits attention. We found that certain CDs derived from a nitrogen-containing precursor (phthalocyanine) in suitable media could provide significant advantages. Excitation-independent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared through facile one-pot hydrothermal (W-CDs) and solvothermal (E-CDs) methods. Compared to W-CDs, ethanol-mediated E-CDs provide narrower particle size distribution, higher pyrrolic and pyridinic N-content, higher quantum yield (
Q Y
E - C D s
/
Q Y
W - C D s
= 2.58), strong green luminescence, excellent biocompatibility and efficient two-photon absorption. The E-CDs enabled superior NIR excitable two-photon bioimaging of breast cancer cells at variable depths and fluorescence quenching (81% extinction) for 10–600 μM Fe3+ ions. The mechanism of sensing was elucidated based on the fluorescence lifetime and the Stern-Volmer formalism. Intracellular Fe3+ ion sensing was first confirmed with E-CDs for lysed MCF-7 cells and quenching was detected in direct proportion to Fe3+ concentration (0–80 μM test range) with an LOD of 10.8 nM. Next, MCF-7 cells supplemented with variable concentrations of Fe3+ were incubated with E-CDs for sensing intracellular Fe3+ in live cells. The E-CDs showed high sensitivity for sensing endogenous Fe3+ ions in a stress-induced cell-based model.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanometric luminescent materials with heterogeneous optical and electronic properties have attracted intense attention for deployment in a range of applications, such as energy-efficient displays, lighting, photovoltaics, and environmental and biomedical probes [1]. Among luminescent nanomaterials, including semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), dye-doped nanoparticles, lanthanides, and polymer dots, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged with excellent potential for bio-applications [2], since most other nanomaterials suffer from limitations of cytotoxicity, photobleaching, low stability, and lack of tunability [2,3]. The unique biological properties of CDs make them suitable for bio-imaging, biosensing and drug delivery compared to traditional quantum dots.

Blue emissive CDs have been predominantly investigated for tissue imaging using one-photon fluorescent probes that are affected by cell autofluorescence. Therefore, synthesizing CDs with two-photon absorption response and emission in red and NIR range can allow deep-tissue penetration (to 1000 μm) with elimination of photodamage and photobleaching [4–6]. It was also reported that appropriate adjustments in the excitation and emission wavelengths can maximize the light penetration through tissues by minimizing absorbance and autofluorescence [6,7]. Further, excitation-independent two-photon fluorescent CDs have attracted growing interest for down-conversion and up-conversion bio-imaging because unwanted tissue autofluorescence can be avoided [6,8,9]. To obtain suitable CDs, the selection of appropriate precursors and synthesis medium and processing are of paramount importance [10,11].

Determining Fe3+ concentration in the human body is crucial, since metal ions and especially iron plays important roles in various biological functions due to its strong redox capacity [12–15]. The total cellular iron concentration is typically 0.3 ± 0.1 mM; however, iron overload or paucity causes severe cell damage and various diseases [15]. Thus, fabricating highly sensitive and selective ferric ion probes is a high priority. Several methods have been developed for Fe3+ detection including fluorescence spectroscopy [16]. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus [13] by hydrothermal method achieved a 900 nM limit of detection (LOD). Nanocomplex formation due to the interaction of amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl functional groups with ferric ions, causing charge/electron transfer between the CDs and Fe3+ has been attributed to the fluorescence quenching via static and dynamic mechanisms. Luminescent copper nanoclusters have also been used for Fe3+ sensing with 80% quenching efficiency and LOD of 25 nM [17]. Apparently. Static quenching operates at low Fe3+concentration, while dynamic mechanism is activated at higher concentrations.

In this work, excitation-independent water-based (W-CDs) and ethanol-based (E-CDs) carbon dots were prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal/solvothermal approach using nitrogen-containing phthalocyanine as a precursor. Compared with W-CDs, E-CDs contained significant fractions of N-functional groups leading to the formation of polyaromatic structures which was shown to produce high-intensity emission. Also, E-CDs showed higher quantum yield (QY), fluorescence lifetime, green emission maxima, and two-photon absorption response. E-CDs were tested for sensitivity and selectivity in detecting Fe3+ions in mixtures containing a range of metal ions at variable concentrations. The sensing mechanism was elucidated using the Stern-Volmer formalism and fluorescence lifetime data. The E-CDs were finally assessed as fluorescent nanoprobes for intracellular sensing of endogenous Fe3+ in MCF-7 cells.

Carbon dots were prepared via both hydrothermal and solvothermal methods using one-step synthesis processes. Initially, 15 mg of phthalocyanine (Sigma-Aldrich, Australia) was dissolved in 80 mL of anhydrous ethanol (Sigma-Aldrich, Australia) and 80 mL of deionized (DI) water separately. Subsequently, the solutions were transferred to Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave chambers (240 mL each) and heated to 220 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solutions were evaporated in an oven at 90 °C for 15 min. The CDs were then dispersed in specified amounts of DI water. The products were passed through 0.22 μm cellulose acetate filters to remove large carbon clusters. The CDs were then subjected to ultrafiltration via dialysis tubes (MWCO 30KDa) for 15 min at 4000 rpm. Finally, the mixtures were dried at 60 °C using a rotary evaporator to obtain solid powders. Table S1 summarizes the process conditions for our sample preparation and the corresponding sample codes.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using JEOL JEM-2100 (USA), operating at 200 kV to determine the size and size distribution of CDs. Prior to TEM analysis, the samples were dropped on a Cu grid, coated with ultrathin amorphous carbon film, and then dried under ambient conditions. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet 6700 (ThermoFisher, USA) over the range 3900–550 cm−1. To study the components and surface properties of the synthesized CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250XI, USA) using a monochromatic Al-K X-ray source (h = 1486.68 eV). Raman spectra were measured using a Perkin Elmer (RamanStation 400, USA) dispersive Raman spectrometer. UV–vis absorption was recorded using Cary60 ultraviolet-visible-near-infrared (UV–vis-NIR) spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed using a Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Scientific, USA). A confocal microscope with pulsed laser was used for investigating the PL lifetime decay, two-photon absorption and emission behavior of synthesized CDs (Leica SP5 II confocal microscope).

Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CDs was determined by comparing the integrated photoluminescence (PL) intensities with quinine sulfate as the standard (QY of 54%) in 0.1 M H2SO4. The absorbance of the CDs in aqueous media and the reference sample was kept below 0.10 at 320 nm to avoid inner filter effects. The QY of the samples were estimated from:

Q Y
( X )
=
Q
Y
s tan d a r d
(
A
s t a n d a r d
A x
)
(
F
x
F
s t a n d a r d
)
(
ƞ
ƞ
s t a n d a r d
)
2
where the subscript “x” and “standard” refer to CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively; A is absorbance at the excitation wavelength; F is integrated emission under fluorescence spectrum; ƞ is the refractive index of the solvent.

The cellular cytotoxicity of E-CDs was tested by means of (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. Briefly, breast cancer cells (MCF-7) were incubated with different concentration of CDs (100–500 μg/mL) for 24 h with a cell density of 40k cells/well. For cytotoxicity measurements, the formazan crystals formed by the live cells were dissolved and subsequently quantified from the absorbance data at 550 nm. Bioimaging was conducted by seeding the cells in 6-well sterile chamber slides in a 500 μL culture medium at a cell density of 40k cells/well. After overnight incubation, the media was replaced with culture media containing the CDs at 500 μg/mL and incubated for 24 h. The culture media was discarded from the sterile chambers, and the cells were washed twice with 1.0 mL phosphate buffer solution (PBS) [18–20]. Subsequently, the cells were fixed with 80% ethanol and imaged by a Leica SP5 II (USA) confocal microscope with mercury lamp as the light source.

The detection of Fe3+ was performed in a HEPES buffer solution at room temperature. In a typical procedure, 200 μL of the E-CD containing aqueous solution (200 μg/mL) was added to 2.8 mL of the HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) in a spectrophotometer quartz cuvette to form the probe solution. Then 10 μL of a known concentration of Fe3+ (10–600 μM) stock solution was added to the cuvette. The cuvette was vibrated for 5 min to form a homogenous mixture. For the selectivity test, a solution of specified concentration of metal ions was added to the prepared E-CDs solution (200 μg/mL). The fluorescence spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 360–600 nm with excitation at 307 nm.

Initially, MCF-7 cells were released by cell lysis in the HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) followed by addition of 200 μL of E-CDs in aqueous media (200 μg/mL). Specific amounts of the solution was placed on the microplate reader, and varied concentrations of metal ion (0, 25, 50, 100 μM) were added to each well. The fluorescence spectra were recorded in the range of 395–515 nm with excitation at 307 nm using BMG LABTECH CLARIOstar spectrometer.

Bioimaging was performed in the cultured MCF-7 cells in the presence of variable Fe3+ concentration (20–100 μM). MCF-7 cells were cultured as noted in Section 2.4; however, the culture media contained 200 μg/mL of E-CDs. Experiments to asses Fe3+ uptake with varied concentrations were performed for another 1 h in the same medium supplemented with 20, 40, 60, and 100 μM Fe3+. The cells were then washed three times with PBS solution (10 mM, pH 7.4) and fixed with 80% ethanol. The two-photon confocal fluorescence imaging was performed with a Leica SP5 II (USA), and confocal image processing was performed using Leica LAS-X software (USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of endogenous Fe3+ ions in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: MCF-7 cells were pre-treated with 200 μg/mL of E-CDs for 24 h, followed by the addition of ascorbic acid (AA) (1.0 mM) for 1 h. Next, the cells were treated with 10 μg/mL of Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) and live cells were imaged over 40 min at 10 min intervals using Leica SP5 II (USA), equipped with a bench top incubator (5% CO2 – 37 °C).

As shown in Fig. 1 the TEM image and size distribution (diameter) of both CDs were uniform with relatively narrow size distributions. The average sizes of the sphere-shaped W-CDs and E-CDs were measured as 2.4 and 2.0 nm, respectively. The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 a) show the functional groups on the surface of the synthesized materials. The presence of CO, CO, and OH bonds confirm the formation of CDs [18]. The broad absorption band in the range of 3600–3000 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of OH and NH [12] on the surface of the E-CDs. The peaks at 2940 and 2880 cm−1 are attributed to the CH bonds [21,22] and the bands at 1400 and 1300 cm−1 is assigned to CH vibrations [23]. We observed CO and CC vibrations at 1720 and 1650cm−1 [24], stretching vibrations of CO at 1210 and 1040 cm−1, and the stretching and bending vibrations of CN at 1100 cm−1 [18,25]. These results indicate that the surface of E-CDs is surrounded by amino groups. The FT-IR spectra of W-CDs show the formation of the designated bonds for CDs at 3000 cm−1 (OH), 1700 cm−1 (CO), and 1000 cm−1 (CO). We note that the FT-IR pattern of W-CDs shows partial similarity with the characteristic peaks of phthalocyanine (PHTH) [26]. This is because of the low solubility of PHTH in water which resulted in the remaining unreacted PHTH within the structure; however, applying high temperature and pressure during the hydrothermal process may lead to partial CD formation. On the other hand, the high electronegativity of oxygen and hydroxyl groups in ethanol and the hydrogen bonding with PHTH, resulted in complete solubility and carbonizing reactions in a homogeneous medium during the synthesis [27,28]. The Raman spectra of both CDs exhibited the characteristic G and D bands at around 1650 and 1350 cm-1, respectively (Fig. 2b). The G and D bands are related to the in-plane vibration of the sp2 hybridized carbon and sp3 defects, respectively [29].

To confirm the chemical composition of the CDs, and their carbon binding and nitrogen configurations, the XPS spectra of the samples were measured. As shown in Fig. 3 (a and b), the survey scans of E-CDs and W-CDs display three dominant peaks corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s in both CDs, which denote that no significant contaminant exists on the surface of the samples. The five typical peaks at 284.8, 285.6, 286.5, 287.8, and 288.8 eV in the C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3c and d) are attributed to the graphitic sp2 C (CC or CC), sp3 C (CN and CO), carbonyl C (CO or CN), and carboxylate C(O)O.

The N1S peaks at 399.75 (399.8 or 399.7 eV) and 401.2 eV (401.1 or 401.3 eV), shown in Fig. 3(e and f), illustrate the presence of nitrogen mostly in the form of sp3 N, CN (Pyridinic) or N-H (Pyrrolic) forms, respectively. The surface atomic concentrations of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen are reported in Table 1 . The nitrogen in E-CDs is 10.5 at%, which is greater by a factor of 2.2 compared to that in W-CDs (4.78 at%). Also, the strong nitrogen signal of E-CDs (Fig. 3a) indicates the successful nitrogen self-doping of E-CDs owing to the high solubility and reactivity of PHTH in ethanol.

UV-vis and PL spectroscopies were conducted to evaluate the optical characteristics of the CDs. The W-CDs emitted blue while the E-CDs emitted intense green fluorescence when excited by 312 nm UV light (inset Fig. 4 a). Both CDs showed an absorption peak at around 230 nm originating from the carbon core which is ascribed to the ᴨ-ᴨ* transition of aromatic CC bonds [30]. The absorption peak at 280 and 330 nm from W-CDs and E-CDs, respectively, correspond to the to n-ᴨ* transition of the CO bond. However, the redshift in the absorption spectra of E-CDs may be attributed to the presence of defect surface states induced by high proportion of N atoms [31,32]. We expected greater redshift for W-CDs in the low-energy region as it was synthesized in a solvent with high polarity. However, poor solubility and aggregation of W-CDs in the solvent precluded the redshift in the W-CDs absorption peaks [11].

Fig. 4(b and c) present PL emission spectra of the aqueous solutions of W-CDs and E-CDs with excitation at 290 and 320 nm, respectively. The results showed significant differences between the CDs at low excitation wavelengths. Fig. 4b shows an increase in emission intensity by increasing the excitation from 290 to 310 nm with a PL maxima of W-CDs at 455 nm and Stokes shift of 145 nm. The fluorescence intensity in the range of 300–700 nm shows a consistent decreasing profile with increasing excitation wavelength up to 420 nm. This behavior can be seen within the three-dimensional excitation-emission fluorescence spectra plots of the W-CDs (Fig. S1a, ESI). Fig. 4c shows three sharp peaks at 355, 507, and 685 nm with excitation of 320 nm. Meanwhile, the photoluminescent excitation (PLE) spectrum of E-CDs shows three peaks at 257, 338 and 398 nm, respectively (Fig. 4d). These results indicate the presence of three main types of excitation energy traps on the surface of E-CD [8].

The peaks at 335 and 685 nm show negligible redshift with an increase in excitation wavelength from 320 to 330 nm. However, by increasing the excitation from 330 to 340 nm (the maximum PL emission), the emission peaks appear at 335 and 685 nm, which decrease and disappear at higher excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4c and Fig. S1b, ESI). E-CDs showed maximum PL emission at 507 nm and a Stokes shift of 167 nm. From the PLE, we conclude that all excitation energy traps are activated at lower excitation wavelengths, while one of the emission centers remained active at higher wavelengths (excitation-independent PL at 507 nm). Thus, both W- and E-CDs exhibit significant excitation-independent properties. This behavior is related to the monotonous surface state defects which may have resulted from the uniform pressure and temperature during the synthesis. Such properties are desirable for downconversion and upconversion fluorescence during cell imaging with low autofluorescence [8,9].

The up-converted emission results show the capability of E-CDs for the simultaneous absorption of two photons (Fig. 4e); however, this is not applicable for W-CDs. Fig. 4e shows that the maximum emission peak for E-CDs remains unchanged at 520 nm in the excitation range of 720–840 nm. However, the emission intensity varies with the excitation wavelength, and the highest emission intensity is observed at the excitation wavelength of 780 nm.

Interestingly, E-CDs show excitation-independent properties in the wavelength range of 710 to 830 nm with a maximum emission at 520 nm (Fig. 4e inset). While the main peak position remains unchanged, on increasing the excitation wavelength from 850 to 890 nm, a second peak appears at around 600 nm, indicating the presence of a secondary two-photon active emission center. These results demonstrate the tunability of emission wavelengths of E-CDs by altering the excitation wavelength for desired outcomes [33]. To verify whether the E-CDs possess two-photon absorption ability, variations in fluorescence intensity and excitation laser power were investigated. Fig. 4f clearly shows the quadratic relationship between the excitation laser power and fluorescence intensity, which confirms that the E-CDs are capable of two- photon NIR excitation [18,19].

The two-photon fluorescence intensity of a fluorophore depends on its emission quantum yield (QY) and two-photon absorption cross-section (σ2p). By using fluorescein (FL) as the reference sample (σFL = 37 GM at 780 nm) [16], the two-photon absorption cross-section (TPA) of E-CDs (σE-CDs) was calculated at 780 nm excitation wavelength. We measured the two-photon emission intensities of E-CDs (FE-CDs) and fluorescein (FFL) at known concentrations (CE-CDs and CFL) and then the TPAs of our probes were estimated as follows [16,17]:
σ
E - C D s
=
σ
F L
F
E - C D
s
F
F L
Q
Y
F L
Q
Y
E - C D s
C
F L
C
E - C D s

The E-CDs showed high two-photon absorption cross section of approximately 53,000 GM (Goeppert-Mayer unit, with 1 GM = 10−50 cm4s/photon) which is higher or comparable to other two-photon fluorescent nanomaterials [18]. The high QY and σ2p of E-CDs make it an excellent candidate for two-photon fluorescence imaging and biosensing [10].

Table 2 shows the fluorescence parameters of both the CDs and the lifetimes of 1.42 ns and 3.7 ns for W-CDs and E-CDs, respectively. The QY of W-CDs and E-CDs were measured as 21.08% and 54.40%, respectively (quinine sulfate reference with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm). Also, the formula below shows that the fluorescence quantum yield is directly proportional to fluorescence lifetime:
Q Y =
k f
/ k
=
τ /
τ r
≈ τ
where kf is the rate of fluorescence, k the rate constant of all deexcitation processes, τ the fluorescence lifetime and τr the radiation lifetime.

The QY values we obtained are higher than or at least comparable to those reported in the literature [8,23,24]. The significant enhancement of QY for E-CDs relative to W-CDs can be attributed to the presence of a higher percentage of nitrogen atoms in E-CDs leading to efficient electron trapping on the surface [34]. In particular, the presence of pyrrolic and pyridinic N leads to the formation of polyaromatic structures by breaking the conjugated carbon structure to produce stronger emission [28]. The XPS spectra show that 7.7% of nitrogen in E-CDs is pyridinic compared to 3.05% in W-CDs. The pyridinic N is able to contribute one electron to the π system across the C atoms (delocalized electron) and may lead to the narrower energy gap between π and π*, thereby increasing the probability of electron excitation at the same excitation energy [35]. In contrast, particle aggregation in W-CDs may quench the fluorescence by resonance energy transfer or ᴨ-ᴨ interactions [11].

The ability of E-CDs for two-photon bioimaging was tested via in vitro cell imaging using MCF-7 cell lines with multiphoton fluorescence microscopy. Fig. 5 shows strong fluorescence of the MCF-7 cells after incubation with E-CDs (500 μg/mL) for 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2 [18–20]. The images were collected with a confocal microscope equipped with a pulsed laser beam operating at 780 nm. Fig. 5a shows that cells treated with E-CDs displayed clear green color at 780 nm excitation with fluorescence mostly in the membrane and cytoplasmic area. The two-photon absorption capability of the E-CDs in the NIR range (780 nm) and emission in the visible range not only increases the depth of imaging but also reduces phototoxicity and autofluorescence at higher wavelengths [5,36]. We investigated the excitation independency of E-CDs through two-photon bioimaging. For efficient imaging, a fluorophore should be excited at its maximum absorption peak (MAP). In an imaging setup using a single fluorophore, this MAP should match the available excitation source. The excitation-independent property of our probe allows it to be used with different excitation sources without shift in the emission wavelength (Fig. S2, ESI). Further, the use of a bandpass filter at or near the emission peak of the fluorophore can improve the sensitivity and linear range of the measurements. When an excitation-dependent probe is used, the emission peak shifts on varying the excitation wavelength making it challenging to choose a proper filter. However, in our case, a single filter can be used (regardless of the excitation wavelength) during an imaging setup. These issues become more complicated when two or more fluorochromes with different excitation/emission wavelengths are used as changing the excitation wavelength for the second fluorochrome will cause a shift in the emission peak of the first probe and cause possible bleed-through of the emission signal to the other channels, making it difficult to distinguish emission signals from each fluorochrome [8,36,37]. The excitation-independent property of E-CDs enables it to be used in other applications such as optical and electronic devices and semiconductor-based irradiation light sources [38].
The W-CDs were also investigated for two-photon bioimaging; however, they were not two-photon excitable, and no appreciatable emission signal was detectable.

A z-stack image was collected to precisely locate the z-position of the E-CDs within the cells (Fig. S3, ESI). The fluorescent dots can be seen within the cell cytoplasm by visualizing them on the z-axis from the bottom (0 μm) to the top level (27 μm, Δz = 3 μm). Thus, we are able to confirm that the E-CDs penetrated inside the cell cytoplasm rather than being bound or adsorbed on the MCF-7 cancer cell surface [5]. To ascertain the viability of E-CDs for biomedical applications, the cytotoxicity of the E-CDs was investigated in vitro. The control comparator was the media with cells but without any CDs (0 μg/mL in the plot). The results show cell viability of 90% and higher with E-CD concentrations of up to 500 μg/mL concentration after 24 h exposure (Fig. S4, ESI), which is acceptable for administering such nanomaterials for diagnosis and sensing.

The XPS results show significant nitrogen content in E-CDs and could serve as superior ionic sensing probes [16,31,39]. The selectivity and sensitivity of the synthesized E-CDs were evaluated in the presence of several related metal ions, including Fe3+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Ce3+, Ni2+, Na+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ag+, and Pb2+. It should be noted that a buffered aqueous solution was used for inhibiting the effect of pH variation on sensor selectivity and sensitivity (HEPES, 10 mM, pH 7.0). As shown in Fig. 6 a, E-CDs display 81% fluorescence quenching efficiency (F/F0) with 500 μM Fe3+ concentration in mixtures containing potential interfering ions. No significant interference was observed under identical conditions, indicating high selectivity of E-CDs for detecting Fe3+ ions.

Fig. 6b shows the fluorescence quenching responses of E-CDs with variable concentrations of Fe3+ ions. These results show that the fluorescence intensity of E-CDs was quenched proportionately on increasing the Fe3+ concentration from 5.0 to 600 μM without any appreciable change in the spectral profile. As shown in Fig. 6c, the quenching ratio is linear with respect to Fe3+ concentration (R 2 = 0.997), and the limit of detection (LOD) of Fe3+ was 17.7 nM based on the ratio of the standard deviation of the response to the profile slope (LOD = 3σ/S). These results indicate improved or comparable performance with respect to the recently reported LOD values of 900, 450, 25, and 16 nM [17,40–42].

The fluorescence quenching phenomena with CDs has attracted much speculation in the literature. Firstly, due to the presence of a large number of carboxyl, hydroxyl and nitrogenous groups on the surface of E-CDs, the interaction of Fe3+ ions with these functional groups may lead to increased probability of nonradiative recombination of the excitons. [16,39]. Therefore, the quenching effects occur due to the coordination reaction between the CD functional groups and the metal ions, which leads to increased probability of nonradiative recombination of the excitons. Secondly, the quenching mechanism can be attributed to the nonradiative electron-hole annihilation process between the E-CDs and Fe3+. In particular, the combination of carbon dots and metal ions leads to the splitting of the d orbital of Fe3+ which results in the transfer of the excited state electrons (of E-CDs) to the half-filled 3-d orbitals of Fe3+, culminating in the quenching of CDs (Scheme 1 ) [16,42]. To further investigate the quenching mechanism of Fe3+ on the E-CDs luminescence intensity, the Stern-Volmer equation was investigated. Interestingly, a linear relationship can be seen in the range of 0–180 μM and a positive curvature was obtained with the Stern-Volmer plot by increasing the ion concentration to 600 μM (Fig. 6d), suggesting that both static and dynamic quenching processes may occur during sensing at higher levels of Fe3+ concentrations [43].

The fluorescence lifetime variation with different concentration of Fe3+ (0–180 μM) was investigated. Fig. S6 ESI illustrates the fluorescence lifetime of the E-CDs (200 μg/mL) in the HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) with variable concentration of metal ions. The results show no significant changes in the lifetime for up to 180 μM Fe3+ ions (Table 3 ).

It has been reported that at a lower concentration of Fe3+ ions, the mechanism of the quenching is primarily static [17]. On adding Fe3+ to the media containing E-CDs, chelation between them and quenching occurs due to the high affinity of Fe3+ ions toward the functional groups on the E-CD surface. A ground-state complexation between the E-CDs and Fe3+ ions proceeds, which results in steady-state luminescence quenching and unaltered excited-state lifetime (Table 3 and Fig. S6, ESI). On adding Fe3+ ions further, the interaction between the functional groups of E-CDs and Fe3+ ions gets saturated which increases the molecular association in the mixture (dynamic quenching process). In this case, the mechanism of interaction might be electron transfer between the carbon-dots and the Fe3+ [17]. These interactions cause the partial transfer of the excited state electrons of CDs to the d orbital of Fe3+. As the ferric cation is trivalent, two charges are taken from the 4 s sub-shell and one from the 3d sub-shell, which provide Fe3+ ions with a highly positive charge density. As a result, electrons withdraw from the surface of the E-CDs towards the deficient filled orbitals of Fe3+ and the structural compactness is reduced which is responsible for the fluorescence “turn-off” with E-CDs [17,41]. Thus, the quenching of Fe3+ ions is induced by both static and dynamic mechanisms.

To evaluate whether the E-CD probe is applicable for biosensing, the lysed MCF-7 cells were used as a model to explore intracellular Fe3+detection. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the fluorescence intensity of E-CDs decreased gradually with increase in Fe3+ concentration (0–100 μM) even when the E-CDs were in contact with intracellular compartments. Fig. 7b shows a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of E-CDs and the concentration of Fe3+ ions (R2 = 0.94). The LOD is estimated to be 10.8 nM, which is significantly lower than the maximum cellular iron concentration (0.4 mM).

To evaluate cell bioimaging with Fe3+, the responses of excitation-independent two-photon fluorescent probes were obtained with cultured cells in the presence of variable Fe3+ concentration (20–100 μM). Before imaging, live cells were incubated with 200 μg/mL E-CDs for 24 h at 37 °C in 5% CO2/air environment followed by washing with PBS solution three times [18–20]. Fig. 8 shows the bright-field transmission, fluorescence, and overlay two-photon fluorescent images of the developed E-CD probes in MCF-7 cells with Fe3+ ions at 780 nm excitation. The bright-field transmission of MCF-7 cells demonstrates clear cell images (Fig. 8A–F). From the two-photon confocal microscopy and overlay images, we note that cells treated only with E-CDs display strong fluorescence in the cytoplasm (Fig. 8G and M). From the Fig. 8H and N, it can be seen that the 20 μM exogenous Fe3+ introduced into the E-CDs pretreated MCF-7 the intracellular fluorescence in the cell cytoplasm slightly decreased. By increasing the concentration of the exogenous Fe3+ from 20 to 80 μM, the fluorescence intensity gradually reduced (Fig. 8I–K and O–Q). At 100 μM Fe3+ concentration, almost no intracellular fluorescence is seen in the cytoplasm (Fig. 8L and R). We also calculated the TPA of E-CDs in the presence of 200 μM of Fe3+. The result demonstrated a dramatic decrease in TPA from approximately 53,000 to 28,500 GM after the probe interacted with Fe3+. The gradual decrease of the mean FL intensity of the cells by increasing the Fe3+ concentration from 20 to 100 μM is shown in Fig. S7 ESI. These results reveal that E-CDs are non-toxic, biocompatible and could serve as effective probes for intracellular Fe3+ sensing and imaging.

We performed a stress-induced cell-based model system to study the endogenous Fe3+ ion sensing capability of E-CDs in living MCF-7 cells [41]. The MCF-7 cells were first exposed to Ascorbic Acid (AA) as a stimulant to accumulate the labile iron present in the nonheme forms, followed by treatment with LPS to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS). This leads to a dynamic Fe2+ to Fe3+ conversion, which presents a detection pathway [44–46]. From the two-photon confocal microscopy images (Fig. 9 F and K), we note that the cells treated with E-CDs and AA display strong fluorescence in the cytoplasm. Signals with similar mean intensity were obtained when the cells were treated with only E-CDs, and E-CDs plus AA (Fig. S8, ESI). These results show that the presence of AA within the cells did not cause any observable interference to E-CD emission intensity. Subsequently, MCF-7 cells were treated with LPS (10 μg/mL) and two-photon confocal images were obtained over a span of 40 min at 10 min intervals. Fig. 9L–O, shows that the fluorescence of E-CDs dramatically decreased after 40 min of LPS treatment. This clearly confirmed the applicability of E-CDs as a probe with rapid response for detecting endogenous Fe3+ ions in living cells.

Carbon dots with down- and up-conversion fluorescent characteristics were prepared by one-step hydrothermal and solvothermal processes using nitrogen containing precursor, phthalocyanine. The CDs were characterized comprehensively using the TEM, UV–vis, FTIR, Raman, PL and XPS techniques and their related attributes were investigated using the confocal, lifetime and multiphoton microscopy techniques. The hydrothermally produced carbon dots (W-CDs) displayed blue fluorescence with a QY of 21.1%, while the solvothermally produced carbon dots (E-CDs) displayed intense green fluorescence with a QY of 54.4%. The superior attributes of E-CDs were found to produce excellent results in cytoplasm penetration in cells with two-photon bioimaging and their application as a fluorescence probe for quantitative detection of ferric ions with a wide linear range, high selectivity and a low limit of detection in an aqueous sample, lysed MCF-7 cells and living MCF-7 cells. By taking advantage of the low limit of detection, E-CDs were successfully applied to monitor the dynamic generation of endogenous Fe3+ in living cells. The superior fluorescence and quenching mechanisms of E-CDs were explained using their chemical composition, electronic configuration, Stern-Volmer plots, and changes in the fluorescence lifetime values.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge the assistance received during the use of ACMM (University of Sydney) facilities for spectroscopic measurements and the Bosch Institute (University of Sydney) for the cell-related studies. Authors PL, SM, and AD wish to thank The University of Sydney for the PhD scholarship support.
There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.01.046.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
